learning_outcomes.description: Develop familiarity with the history, beliefs, and practices of Muslims and diversity therein
Develop deeper understanding of Islam as an historical tradition in diverse societies
Examine, discuss, and debate main issues related to Islam
Gain knowledge as to how the Quran and Hadith work to shape Muslim views and practices.
Develop a critical understanding around debates centering on Islam and Muslims
Demonstrate reasoned thought, creativity and a high quality of written expression
Conduct advanced research on Islam independently
Gain awareness of contemporary issues, such as gender and multiculturalism, with respect to Islam.

assessments.task_description:
External students are expected to complete 8/10 diary entries over the semester, to be submitted in via Turnitin in Blackboard. Entries should be 150 words (+/-10%), and demonstrate an engagement with the course material and set readings in line with the stated topic/question found on Blackboard/Library Resources page.

Diary entries are due each week starting in week 2 (due week 3) for 10 weeks (including week 12, if public holiday). Students will find the readings for each diary entries on the Library Resources page (link in Blackboard) for each respective week. Diary entries are due the week after they are assigned by 2pm (e.g. week 2 diary entry is due by 2pm in week 3 on the day of the scheduled lecture). (2.5 marks each)

If 5 or fewer diary entries are completed, the student will receive 0 diary entry marks.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

See late submission below.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date: You must attend this exam in person and at the scheduled time and day.
assessments.task_description: This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
Topic: For this essay, you will need to respond to one of several statements that will be provided on the Blackboard site. The essay should be based on no less than 5 academic sources.

Referencing Style: Students are strongly encouraged to use Chicago style for references and bibliographies: http://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/tools_citationguide/citation-guide-1.html

Cover Sheet: Students must use and complete the provided cover sheet for this essay found in Blackboard. An essay submitted without a completed cover sheet will be graded accordingly, based on the marking criteria. Falsified or misreprestented information on the cover sheet is a breach of the student code of conduct and will be referred to the academic integrity unit.

Word Limits: 2,000 words. Within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. Word count does not include footnotes and bibliography, however, discursive footnotes are included in the word count. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria. 

AI: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Thinking about Islam

In this introductory week we develop a framework for thinking about Islam, moving away from an approach that seeks to define an essence to religion, and encourage a way of thinking about religion that is embedded in local contexts and people’s everyday lives. 


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Thinking about Islam In this introductory week we develop a framework for thinking about Islam, moving away from an approach that seeks to define an essence to religion, and encourage a way of thinking about religion that is embedded in local contexts and people’s everyday lives.

learning_activities.topic:
Dope Mipsters: Conceptualising Islam

Scholars have rigorously debated: 'What is Islam'? This lecture provides a surveys key debates around conceptualizing Islam, and it daws on a case study of Muslim Hip Hop. We consider the influential work of Talal Asad's and his discussion of Islam as a ‘discursive tradition’. His conceptualization of Islam encourages a shift in thinking about Islam as a fixed set of texts, practices or social relationships, to seeing Islam as part of a field of debate and authorisation around what is ‘Islamic’ and what is ‘not Islamic’. Locating our understanding of Islam in an ongoing field of contestation and practice helps us to avoid essentializing Islam, on the one hand, and allows us to pay closer attention to themes of tradition, authority and how to account for change.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06, L07
learning_activities.topic_description: Dope Mipsters: Conceptualising Islam Scholars have rigorously debated: 'What is Islam'? This lecture provides a surveys key debates around conceptualizing Islam, and it daws on a case study of Muslim Hip Hop. We consider the influential work of Talal Asad's and his discussion of Islam as a ‘discursive tradition’. His conceptualization of Islam encourages a shift in thinking about Islam as a fixed set of texts, practices or social relationships, to seeing Islam as part of a field of debate and authorisation around what is ‘Islamic’ and what is ‘not Islamic’. Locating our understanding of Islam in an ongoing field of contestation and practice helps us to avoid essentializing Islam, on the one hand, and allows us to pay closer attention to themes of tradition, authority and how to account for change.

learning_activities.topic:
Representing Islam: Orientalism and beyond

How is ‘Islam’ and how are ‘Muslims’ represented in academic and political discourse and in media? What difference does this make? Drawing on Edward Said’s concept of Orientalism, a founding text in postcolonial studies, we consider the importance of representation in history and in the contemporary context post-9/11.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06, L07, L08
learning_activities.topic_description: Representing Islam: Orientalism and beyond How is ‘Islam’ and how are ‘Muslims’ represented in academic and political discourse and in media? What difference does this make? Drawing on Edward Said’s concept of Orientalism, a founding text in postcolonial studies, we consider the importance of representation in history and in the contemporary context post-9/11.

learning_activities.topic:
Science & Islam

This week we explore the theme of Islam and science from an anthropological perspective, recognizing ‘scientific’ reasoning alongside religious reasoning and the ways in which religion and science interact in the everyday and the connections between Islam and modernity. We explore how Islam relates to local folk beliefs though healing practices and how religion and science connect to a transnational religious reinvigoration movement through the Turkish Gulen movement. The inquiry proposed moves away from standard theological and philosophical debates about the compatibility between ‘science and religion’ to examine how these relationships in everyday life and social and political rhetoric are conceived and what purposes they serve.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06, L07, L08
learning_activities.topic_description: Science & Islam This week we explore the theme of Islam and science from an anthropological perspective, recognizing ‘scientific’ reasoning alongside religious reasoning and the ways in which religion and science interact in the everyday and the connections between Islam and modernity. We explore how Islam relates to local folk beliefs though healing practices and how religion and science connect to a transnational religious reinvigoration movement through the Turkish Gulen movement. The inquiry proposed moves away from standard theological and philosophical debates about the compatibility between ‘science and religion’ to examine how these relationships in everyday life and social and political rhetoric are conceived and what purposes they serve.

learning_activities.topic:
Ethics and Piety

What does it mean to be a ‘good Muslim’? This week we examine notions of Islamic piety and how people strive to live in an Islamic way and the challenges that often ensue. Drawing on questions of ‘everyday ethics’ on ‘how to lead a good life’ and cultivate virtuous dispositions and habits, we examine the seminal works of Charles Hirschkind on the acoustics of virtue formation (listening to cassette sermons) and Saba Mahmood’s examination of how wearing the veil serves as a means to inner transformation. We also examine the difficulties that people face in leading a good Muslim life, drawing on case studies from a high security prison (Williams) and a rural Egyptian town (Schielke). We also consider the meaning and performances of salat (Islamic prayer) and their personal and social significance across a range of contexts from Cairo to Indonesia.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L06, L07

learning_activities.topic:
Islam in Australia: Migrating and Adapting

This lecture surveys the history of Islam in Australia, which predates the European colonial contact. It discusses the diversity of Islam and Muslims in Australia today and some challenges and opportunities. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L06, L07, L08
learning_activities.topic_description: Islam in Australia: Migrating and Adapting This lecture surveys the history of Islam in Australia, which predates the European colonial contact. It discusses the diversity of Islam and Muslims in Australia today and some challenges and opportunities.

learning_activities.topic:
Islam and Gender

The relationship between Islam and gender is an uneasy one from the perspective of secular-liberal politics and in consideration of feminist projects. Islam and gender conjures up associations with the subjugation of women and male patriarchy viewed as part of a ‘cultural backwardness’. This week we consider some case studies where gender norms are challenged, from an Egyptian mosque movement of women freely donning the veil in pursuit of piety, to gender roles in modern Iran and Muslim women in Malaysia starting a global movement for equality.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L06, L07

learning_activities.topic:
Midterm exam

All students, including those completing the course by delayed viewing or by external mode, are required to complete the midterm exam in person. The exam will be held in week 8 and in place of the lecture. You must apply and receive approval for completing the deferred exam to be held one week later (see below).

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L06, L07, L08
learning_activities.topic_description: Midterm exam All students, including those completing the course by delayed viewing or by external mode, are required to complete the midterm exam in person . The exam will be held in week 8 and in place of the lecture. You must apply and receive approval for completing the deferred exam to be held one week later (see below).

learning_activities.topic:
Islam and Law

In this lecture we look at Islamic law also known as Shari'a law as a legal system that has its foundation in the Quran and that has been practicsed by Muslims since the early days of Islam in the 7th Century. The implementation of Shari'a law by many Muslim-majority countries in recent decades presents a case for the incompatability of Shari'a law with contemporary social expectations. This lecture uses Malaysia as a case study to shed light into Islamic law evolultion, development and change paying attention to the legacy of colonisation on the type of Islamic law inherited by the postcolonial state and used to regulate citizenship, gender and religious life.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L06, L07, L08

learning_activities.topic:
Deferred Midterm Exam

The deferred exam is scheduled for week 9, Monday 21 September at 12:00 noon, room TBC. You must have advance approval for missing the originally scheduled exam. You must attend the deferred exam date unless your original absence covers the period for this deferred exam (that is, you have an approved deferral period that extends longer than 1 week from the original exam date).

learning_activities.topic:
Public Holiday

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06, L07, L08

Race, Ethnicity and Islam

This week we examine how seemingly ready-made categories of identity, including nationality, ethnicity, and religion, are not static properties of individuals but are better thought of as emerging through relationships and processes. Through local contexts and examinations of everyday life we will examine how boundaries are formed and how national belonging, race, ethnicity and religion become entangled in complex and contested ways. We explore the case of white German converts to Islam and how national allegiances are questioned and ethnic boundaries are seemingly crossed. We also examine racialization in Australia and the UK and Blackamerican Islam. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06, L07
learning_activities.topic_description: Race, Ethnicity and Islam This week we examine how seemingly ready-made categories of identity, including nationality, ethnicity, and religion, are not static properties of individuals but are better thought of as emerging through relationships and processes. Through local contexts and examinations of everyday life we will examine how boundaries are formed and how national belonging, race, ethnicity and religion become entangled in complex and contested ways. We explore the case of white German converts to Islam and how national allegiances are questioned and ethnic boundaries are seemingly crossed. We also examine racialization in Australia and the UK and Blackamerican Islam.

learning_activities.topic:
Mobilizing

This week examines how Muslims have been active in seeking social change. We examine how Islam is related to broader social and political movements, before turning to more vexing issues around the relationship between Islam and violence. Using the literature from religion and violence, we seek to encourage broader thinking about Islam and violence in view of contemporary issues of terrorism, asking what definition of religion is assumed in the discourse around ‘religious violence’.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L08
